summary: Mosaic will launch with curated health and productivity widgets as premium features, not broad third-party ecosystem.

cmo.description: Content, creative direction, brand. Obsessed with offers, value equations, and volume.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Today we're tackling the Mosaic plugin ecosystem strategy - what third-party integrations and open-source widgets we include beyond our core verticals. I've got some data points: we have CVS-003 through CVS-011 already covering the technical architecture and core widget extraction from Lever, but we need to define our broader widget strategy. The question is scope - do we go wide with health, productivity, home automation, or stay focused?

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Hold on. Before we start cataloging every possible widget, what's the user story here? Are we building a dashboard for power users who want to customize everything, or are we creating a curated experience that just works? Because those are fundamentally different products.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Steve's hitting the key point. From a positioning perspective, if we're "the everything dashboard," we're competing with Notion and every other productivity tool. But if we're "the life optimization hub for high performers," that's a different conversation entirely. What's our wedge?

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: The technical architecture in CVS-003 and CVS-004 gives us flexibility, but we need to be realistic about maintenance overhead. Every widget we include is code we have to support, update, and debug. I'd rather have 10 bulletproof widgets than 50 mediocre ones.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Exactly. And here's what concerns me - we're talking about weather widgets and calendar integrations when we haven't even proven product-market fit with our core offering. We're solving for problems we don't know users have.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Fair point, but we need to think strategically about ecosystem lock-in. If Mosaic becomes the place where people manage their entire digital life, that's massive retention value.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Theo, that's the trap though. "Everything for everyone" is nothing for no one. Look at what actually drives revenue - it's not having 100 widgets, it's having the 3-5 widgets that people can't live without. What are those going to be for our audience?

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: From a technical standpoint, I think we start with APIs, not widgets. Build the plugin architecture robust enough that third parties can extend it, then see what the community actually wants. Don't pre-optimize for use cases we're guessing at.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: That's backwards, Elon. We need to have an opinion about what matters. If we just throw up an API and hope developers figure it out, we'll end up with a fragmented mess. What are the 5-7 categories that actually complement finance, faith, and family?

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: I'm with Steve on this one. Health and fitness are obvious - they're part of the optimization mindset. Maybe sleep tracking since that impacts everything else. But home automation? That's a different buyer persona entirely.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Let me reframe this. What if we think about it in tiers? Core widgets we build and maintain, verified partner widgets we curate, and then an open ecosystem for everything else?

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: That could work technically. The widget interface from CVS-004 could have different security and performance tiers. Core widgets get deep system integration, partner widgets get standard APIs, community widgets run in sandboxes.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: The tiered approach has merit, but we still need to define what goes in each tier. For core, I'd say health metrics that tie into our optimization narrative - sleep, fitness, maybe nutrition. Not weather, not home automation.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Health makes sense because it amplifies our core value prop. If someone's tracking their net worth, their faith practice, and their family relationships, adding health completes the "whole life optimization" story. That's premium positioning.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: So are we converging on health and fitness as the first expansion category? What about productivity tools - habits, journaling, meditation?

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Those feel more natural to me than weather widgets. Journaling could actually enhance the faith component. Habit tracking supports the optimization mindset. But we need to be selective - maybe one strong widget per category rather than five mediocre options.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: If we're going health and productivity first, I need to extend CVS-004 to define security models for third-party data. Health data especially needs encryption and user consent flows built into the API design.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: And we need to think about the business model here. Are these widgets customer acquisition tools, retention plays, or revenue drivers? Because that changes everything about which ones we prioritize.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good question. My gut says retention and engagement first, revenue second. If Mosaic becomes sticky enough that people can't leave, the monetization opportunities follow.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: I disagree. If we're building premium features, they should drive premium revenue from day one. Free widgets commoditize the platform. Curated, high-value widgets justify subscription pricing.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Both perspectives work, but we need to pick one. I lean toward Steve's approach - start premium, prove value, then expand. It's easier to go downmarket than upmarket.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: From an implementation standpoint, we could build the core health and habit widgets in-house as premium features, then open the API for community extensions. Gives us control over the core experience while enabling broader ecosystem growth.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Alright, I think we have enough to move forward. Let me summarize the converged approach: start with health and productivity widgets as premium features, build them in-house for quality control, and design the API architecture to support broader third-party ecosystem later. Steve and Alex, I need you to define the specific widget priorities. Elon, extend the technical architecture to support this tiered approach.

actionItems.text: Define priority health and productivity widgets for Mosaic launch
Extend CVS-004 to include tiered widget architecture and security models
Develop premium widget monetization and positioning strategy
Design widget security and data privacy framework

topPriorities: Define core health and productivity widget specifications
Design tiered widget architecture with security models
Establish premium positioning for curated widget experience

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
